sir?

think that's the best way to go about it.

thank you.

content.

listening.

did a rudimentary perplexity integration where we would just get the major highlights of what is correct, what is not, and have it highlight the parts that need to still be double-checked.

I look out, I call them AI-isms.
I think that's a big part.
So it's...
It's AI, especially if use the same models, and if you don't put high quality prompts, it'll tend to use some cliched language.
It'll tend to repeat itself.
If we're working with keywords, I have to look out that it's not just ham- them in there.
They need to be in there naturally, right?
So these kinds of things definitely tip me off.
Maybe we need to go back, iterate again, or maybe really clarify our starting prompts.
Yeah, that makes sense.

We were delivering around 1 million words per month.

It was a project where we were editing machine-translated content from Chinese web novels.
So it would be machine-translated, and our editors would have to really go in there.
I think it was also one of the more complex projects because before I started this project, I've never heard of web novels.
Which is like this new, you know, it's very popular in China.
But imagine like a book that just keeps going and going and going and going and doesn't end.
It's like 600, 700 chapters, you know.
So other than just, you know, really looking at these very specific like Chinese idioms, we needed to understand Chinese idioms, how they can be mistranslated.
We also needed to take out sensitivities are different in China and in America.
This is for an American audience.
We had to edit for that.
And also just keep a...
Big spreadsheet of how every character is related to each other, what they did to each other, what they said to each other, just plot points.

Because, you know, after 600 chapters, even the writer themselves forgets that, oh, this character actually, you know, doesn't know this other character, or they never met, or they met a long time ago, and now they're meeting again, you know.
So, like, these kinds of things, you really had to keep a vast spreadsheet of all the character connections.
I'd say that's one of my biggest projects.
Yeah, it was very interesting.

yeah.

some that I think are in the ballpark, but...

happy to ask you.
Yeah, I was just reading through the job description.
I was wondering how much of the managing editor role is designing prompts versus reviewing and refining the outputs that we get.
Good question.
I'd say it's variable for week to week, because if your pipelines are in good order and they're delivering content that's high quality, it's when you're It's They're
And you have the ability to get something out of the pipeline that you can edit within a handful of minutes and feel that it's ready for client delivery.
Then there's not much prompt engineering to do.
However, if the pipeline is not in that state, I think your job skews much more heavily into, hey, what inputs are we giving the pipeline?
And how can we improve those inputs so that I have less work to do?
Like I do view an inherent part of the job to make the job easier every day.
Like there's no reason that it should feel just as tough to deliver for a client on day one as it is by day 30 or 60, et cetera, because we should be building efficiency for ourself.
That's the whole reason.
Right, right.
So I would say that it is an integral part of the job.
If you want to give it a default value, it's probably like 70, 30 at the beginning.
And flip that, you know, closer to the end.
Where, and I want to, I'm lumping together, um, prompt engineering and context engineering, obviously.
They're separate, but interrelated.
And that is like the other part of the job.

That's great.

Let's say you're getting a substandard output as an example.

You can look at, for instance, the engineered context that we've given it to say, hey, have we given it an example of the kind of CTA that we want?
Have we shown, you know, LLMs do great with representative examples.

We do not want you to copy these exactly.
Exactly right.

prompts, and then they leverage artifacts, a.k.a.
context.

And they, the, I think that it's important to, like, contextualize that the artifact, or the prompt engineering and context engineering part of it is looked at as a way for you to build leverage.
So there is no, like, and I only say this because it is present in some editorial organizations or content organizations.
There is no inherent, like, higher value given.
To manual work.
Right, okay.
In some organizations, and I'm sure you've seen it, it's like, but how many hours did you personally work on ContentX?
Right, right.

How many words were changed?
Like, what are the dips?

Like, it is, hey, how good are you at building leverage for yourself by reducing the amount of money?
Manual labor you have.
There is no stigma attached to that.

The long-term, okay, you published it three weeks ago.
How is it doing now?
Like, is this delivering to goal?
And if not, is it just the wrong content to have made?
Or can we put it in some way, refresh that, et cetera?
And so, like, I think that's an important way to look at the...
Context engineering and prompting work that is involved in the process.
It's like that is the labor you use so that you can deliver at scale.

yeah, yeah,
